lesson.menu_title: Difficult Conversations -- Giving Clear, Kind Feedback
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Difficult Conversations & Giving Feedback
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 18 -- Difficult Conversations

listenings.text: Good feedback is not about blame. It is about helping someone grow. Last week I had to address a real problem with a developer on my team. In our one-on-one I stayed calm and I started with the facts, not the person. I said the last two releases were late, and I acknowledged that the workload had been heavy. Then I asked for his perspective, because his side of the story always matters. He told me the test environment had been broken for days, and that nobody had reported it. So the delay was not really his fault. I reassured him that I was on his side, and we agreed on a clear next step together. He said he would flag any blocker early from now on, and I told him I would protect his focus time. The key is simple: describe the behavior, never attack the person. When people feel respected, they stay open, they stay accountable, and they actually change. A hard conversation, handled with care, builds trust instead of fear.

Hi Rafael, this is Sofia from the program office. I wanted to follow up on the feedback workshop you ran for the new leads. The team loved it. One of them told me it was the most useful session of the quarter, and another said she finally felt ready to handle a tough conversation. They especially liked your rule: address the behavior, not the person. The leadership team asked me to tell you that your approach will be shared across all the squads next month. One small request: could you write a one-page guide on giving feedback, with three example phrases? They said it should be short and practical. The deadline is next Friday. Thank you again, Rafael. People keep telling me how calm and fair you are in difficult moments. Talk soon.

extra_audio.text: First, I described the behavior with the facts, not the person. Next, I acknowledged that the workload had been heavy. Then I asked for his perspective and listened to his side. After that, I reassured him that I was on his side. Finally, we agreed on a clear next step, and he said he would flag any blocker early.
